key_points: The East India Company gradually transformed from a trading organization into a political power by using military strength, diplomacy and economic control.
The Battle of Plassey (1757) allowed the Company to gain influence over Bengal after the defeat of Siraj-ud-Daulah with the help of Mir Jafar.
The Battle of Buxar (1764) further strengthened British power and resulted in the Company receiving the Diwani rights from Mughal emperor Shah Alam II.
Control of revenue collection in Bengal gave the Company financial resources to expand its army and territories.
British expansion continued through strategic policies such as the Subsidiary Alliance and the Doctrine of Lapse, which weakened Indian rulers and increased British territorial control.
examples: Robert Clive played a major role in the victory of the British in the Battle of Plassey.
The combined forces of Mir Qasim, Shuja-ud-Daulah and Shah Alam II were defeated in the Battle of Buxar.
Tipu Sultan of Mysore resisted British expansion but was defeated in the Anglo-Mysore wars.
The Marathas also fought several wars against the British but were eventually defeated.
formulas: Company transformation → Trade → Political influence → Military victories → Territorial rule
Power consolidation → Battle of Plassey (1757) + Battle of Buxar (1764) + Diwani rights (1765)
Expansion strategy → Military conquest + Political alliances + Annexation policies
exam_tips: Explain how economic control through Diwani strengthened British power.
Mention important battles and their outcomes when answering long questions.
Describe British expansion policies such as Subsidiary Alliance and Doctrine of Lapse clearly.
Use examples of resistance by Indian rulers like Tipu Sultan and the Marathas.
